perturbation.original_text: Each Party, to the extent of its right to do so, shall disclose to the other Party the Proprietary Information which are a ppropriate to fulfil the objectives of this Agreement as set up in the recitals.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall endeavor to disclose to the other Party Proprietary Information it believes might be relevant to the objectives of this Agreement as set up in the recitals.
perturbation.explanation: The original text created a clear obligation to disclose appropriate Proprietary Information. The modified text replaces 'shall disclose' with 'shall endeavor to disclose' and 'which are appropriate' with 'it believes might be relevant', making the disclosure obligation subjective and less enforceable. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the duty to disclose relevant information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly unrelated, intentionally withholding crucial proprietary information under the guise of 'endeavoring' and subjectively determining relevance, could be construed as a restraint of trade if it hinders competition or prevents fair market practices.  The Sherman Act prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade.  A deliberately weakened disclosure agreement could facilitate anti-competitive behavior by allowing a party to conceal information that would otherwise promote competition. The original text sought to prevent information asymmetry, which is vital to avoiding such behaviours. By creating loopholes by weakening the legal obligation, this change risks enabling actions that restrain trade.

Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 1, 26 Stat. 209 ; Aug. 17, 1937, ch. 690, title VIII, 50 Stat. 693 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. L. 94–145, § 2 , Dec. 12, 1975 , 89 Stat. 801 ; Pub. L. 101–588, § 4(a) , Nov. 16, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2880 ; Pub. L. 108–237, title II, § 215(a) , June 22, 2004 , 118 Stat. 668 .) L. 93–528 substituted “a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding one million dollars if a corporation, or, if any other person, one hundred thousand dollars, or by imprisonment not exceeding three years” for “a misdemeanor, and on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding f
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to disclose proprietary information, potentially facilitating anti-competitive behavior. The original text mandated disclosure of 'appropriate' information, while the changed text only requires an 'endeavor' to disclose what 'it believes might be relevant.' This subjectivity could allow a party to withhold information that would otherwise promote competition, contradicting the Sherman Act, as outlined in 15 USC 1, which declares illegal 'every contract, combination...or conspiracy, in restraint of trade.' By weakening the disclosure requirements, the modified contract creates a loophole that could be exploited to restrain trade, even if unintentionally, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: the receiving Party hereby covenants that, during the validity period of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after its end or its termination, the Proprietary Information received from the disclosing Party shall: (a) be protected and kept in strict confidence by the receiving Party which must use the same degree of precaution and safeguards as it uses to protect its own proprietary information of like importance, but in no case any less than reasonable care,
perturbation.changed_text: the receiving Party hereby covenants that, during the validity period of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after its end or its termination, the Proprietary Information received from the disclosing Party shall: (a) be protected and kept in confidence by the receiving Party using precautions it deems necessary,
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear standard for protecting Proprietary Information, linking it to the protection of the receiving party's own information and requiring at least 'reasonable care.' The modified text replaces this with 'using precautions it deems necessary', making the level of protection entirely subjective and potentially inadequate. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modified clause potentially contradicts the Economic Espionage Act (18 U.S. Code § 1832), which criminalizes the theft or misappropriation of trade secrets. By allowing the receiving party to use precautions they 'deem necessary,' the agreement opens the door for inadequate security measures, which could lead to the misappropriation of trade secrets. The original text, by requiring at least reasonable care, provided a basic level of protection against such misappropriation, whereas the edited text lacks such guarantee and thus conflicts with the intent of the Act.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret , that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret , knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains such information; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys such information; (3) receives, buys, or possesses such information, knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 10 years, or both. (b) Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $5,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3489 ; amended Pub. L. 112–236, § 2 , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1627 ; Pub. L. 114–153, § 3(a)(1) , May 11, 2016 , 130 Stat. 382
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with 18 U.S. Code § 1832, which criminalizes the theft or misappropriation of trade secrets. By changing the required level of care from 'reasonable care' to 'precautions it deems necessary,' the agreement introduces a subjective standard that could allow for inadequate protection, increasing the risk of misappropriation. Snippet 2 states that the law applies to whoever 'steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals... such information'. The original text's 'reasonable care' standard offered a basic safeguard against such actions, which is absent in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: (b) be only disclosed to and used by those persons who have a need to know and solely for the purpose specified in this Agreement, within the receiving Party's organisation and to its professional advisors, provided that in this latter case the disclosing Party so notifies the other Party and obtains the authorization from the other Party to make such disclosure, such authorizat ionnot being unreasonably withheld, and provided that such advisor(s) agree(s) to be bound by the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) be disclosed to and used by persons who might benefit from the information within the receiving Party's organization and to its professional advisors.
perturbation.explanation: The original text limits disclosure to those with a 'need to know' and requires authorization for disclosure to professional advisors. The modified text broadens this to anyone who 'might benefit' from the information, removes the authorization requirement for advisors, and removes the clause about said advisors being bound by the terms of the agreement, significantly increasing the risk of unauthorized use and disclosure. The original text provides more control. By broadening disclosure, the intent is undermined.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the contract may not explicitly deal with personal data, the broadened scope of disclosure could inadvertently include personal data, thus putting the agreement at odds with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Article 5 of the GDPR specifies the principles relating to processing of personal data. Specifically, data should be processed in a manner ensuring appropriate security of the personal data, using appropriate technical or organizational measures (Article 5(1)(f)). The original clause has safety measures such as that the recipient must only disclose to those with a 'need to know' in mind. Removing those considerations could be seen as a potential violation of GDPR if the information includes Personally Identifiable Information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens disclosure of information, removing the 'need to know' limitation and authorization requirement. This introduces ambiguity, potentially violating GDPR principles if the information includes personal data, as the Law Explanation suggests. The original contract provides a safer approach by limiting who can see what information. Because the modified text removes the ‘need to know’ clause, there is an increased risk of violating Article 5(1)(f) which ensures appropriate security of personal data. The scraped snippet from GDPR-info.eu describes Article 5 as the ‘Principles relating to processing of personal data’ and suggests that data should be processed ensuring data security. The modified text increases the risk of data breach, violating the original intent and violating the GDPR principles.

perturbation.original_text: (d) neither be disclosed nor caused to be disclosed whether directly or indirectly to any third Party or persons other than t hose mentioned in subparagraph b) or the Buyer Clients or its co -contractors for the Project,
perturbation.changed_text: (d) not be intentionally disclosed to any third Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a broad prohibition on disclosure to third parties. The modified text narrows this to 'intentional' disclosure, creating a loophole for unintentional disclosures. This ambiguity weakens the protection of Proprietary Information, as a party could argue that a disclosure was unintentional and therefore not a breach of the agreement. Furthermore, the phrase removes references to Buyer Clients which could mean the other party can share information, so long as its unintentional.
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly unrelated, if the 'Proprietary Information' inadvertently includes California consumer data, the 'intentional' disclosure clause could create a conflict with the CCPA.  The CCPA grants consumers the right to prevent the sale of their personal information.  If a disclosure of consumer data, even unintentional, were to occur that constituted a 'sale' under the CCPA (which is broadly defined), the 'intentional' loophole would not excuse the violation. The original text offers a more robust safety net by not having that intentionality loophole.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81.5. California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 [1798.100 - 1798.199.100] ( Title 1.81.5 added by Stats. 2018, Ch. 55, Sec. 3. ) 1798.100. General Duties of Businesses that Collect Personal Information (a) A business that controls the collection of a consumer’s personal information shall, at or before the point of collection, inform consumers of the following: (1) The categories of personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of personal information are collected or used and whether that information is sold or shared. A business shall not collect additional categories of personal information or use personal information collected for additional purposes that are incompatible with the disclosed purpose for which the personal information was collected without providing the consumer with notice consistent with this section. (2) If the business collects sensitive personal information, the categories of sensitive personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of sensitive personal information
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by limiting the prohibition of disclosure to 'intentional' acts, introduces a significant loophole. The original text broadly prohibited disclosure, offering greater protection. The CCPA, as outlined in the scraped snippets, aims to give consumers control over their personal information and prevent its sale or sharing. Snippet 2 states that consumers have the right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information. If proprietary information inadvertently includes California consumer data and is disclosed, even unintentionally, and such disclosure constitutes a 'sale' under CCPA (which is broadly defined), the 'intentional' loophole would not excuse the violation. This is because the right to prevent sale or sharing is not contingent on intent. Thus, the modified clause creates a contradiction as it weakens the consumer's protection against the sale or sharing of their data, even if unintentional.

perturbation.original_text: 12. This Contract shall be governed by Buyer country laws and its appropriate courts of Paris. It is expressly agreed that if any dispute should arise, the competent jurisdiction of Paris in France shall be the sole competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. This Contract shall be interpreted under principles of law generally recognized, with disputes ideally resolved through amicable negotiation. Should legal action become necessary, jurisdiction will be determined as appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly designates the Buyer's country laws and the courts of Paris as the governing law and jurisdiction. The modified text replaces this with vague references to 'principles of law generally recognized' and 'jurisdiction will be determined as appropriate', creating significant ambiguity. This makes it difficult to determine which laws apply and where disputes will be resolved, rendering the clause largely unenforceable. The original intent was to resolve disputes as per France's jurisdiction, which is lost now.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modified clause could contradict 28 U.S. Code § 1332, which establishes the requirements for diversity jurisdiction in US federal courts. If a dispute arises between the parties, and the ambiguous jurisdiction clause leads to a US court being asked to hear the case, the court would need to determine if the requirements for diversity jurisdiction are met (i.e., the parties are citizens of different states or countries, and the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000). The original clause provided certainty, ensuring French law applies and excluding US jurisdiction.  The modified clause, by removing this certainty, could open the door to litigation in US federal courts, thus potentially conflicting with the intent to have the contract governed by and adjudicated in France.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura
perturbation.justification: The original contract text specified Buyer country laws and Paris courts for governance and dispute resolution, providing certainty. The changed text introduces ambiguity by referring to 'principles of law generally recognized' and stating that jurisdiction will be 'determined as appropriate'. This contradicts the intent of the original clause, which ensured French law and jurisdiction. The LLM explanation suggests this ambiguity could allow for US federal court jurisdiction under 28 U.S. Code § 1332 if diversity of citizenship and a minimum amount in controversy ($75,000) exist. Scraped Law Snippet 2 confirms that 28 U.S. Code § 1332 grants district courts jurisdiction in cases involving citizens of different states or citizens of a state and foreign subjects, provided the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000. The modified text, by removing the explicit designation of French law and jurisdiction, opens the door to potential litigation in US courts, directly contradicting the original intent and creating a legally significant contradiction.
